topic: Survival in Extreme Cold: The Role of Antifreeze Proteins
title: Mechanisms of Cryoprotection in Arctic Fauna: A Molecular Perspective

lesson.learn: Antifreeze proteins (AFPs) are a family of proteins that play a critical role in the survival of certain Arctic animals by inhibiting ice crystal formation and protecting cellular membranes from damage.
lesson.simpler: Wood frogs (Rana sylvatica) and some fish species, such as Antarctic icefish (Channichthyidae), produce antifreeze proteins that bind to ice crystal surfaces, slowing down their growth and preventing cellular freezing.
lesson.keyTakeaway: The efficacy of antifreeze proteins in preventing cellular damage during rapid freezing is attributed to their unique ability to bind to water molecules and modify the crystallization process, thereby preventing the formation of lethal ice crystals.
lesson.deeperDive: Studies have shown that wood frogs synthesize antifreeze proteins in their liver and secrete them into the bloodstream, where they are transported to various tissues to prevent freezing. The crystal growth inhibition mechanism of these proteins involves the binding of the protein to the ice crystal surface, preventing further growth and subsequent cellular damage. In the case of Antarctic icefish, structural analysis has revealed that their antifreeze proteins exhibit a similar repeat sequence to those found in wood frogs, highlighting the convergent evolution of this adaptation in different species.
lesson.funFact: Wood frogs and some fish species have evolved remarkable strategies to survive freezing conditions, including the ability to rapidly freeze their bodily fluids and then reabsorb them when temperatures rise.

people: Professor of Biochemistry at the University of Washington
Research Scientist at the University of Toronto
Professor of Biology at Iowa State University
years: 1968 - Discovery of antifreeze proteins in fish by DeVries et al. (Science 162, 1259-1261)
1995 - Identification of similar proteins in wood frogs by Storey and Storey (Science 268, 118-121)
2015 - Study on the genetic basis of antifreeze protein production in icefish by Himmelberger et al. (PLoS ONE 10, e0140135)
